network.overall_strategy: The synthesis network routes simple volcanic and atmospheric feedstocks (CO₂, H₂, N₂, CH₄, H₂S, NH₃) through hydrothermal carbon fixation and surface photochemistry to generate the key precursors (HCN, formaldehyde, 3-aminopropanal) required for the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic pyrrolidine construction pathway. The core 5-step cyanosulfidic route—cyanohydrin formation, H₂S-mediated thiolation-cyclization, Cu/H₂S reductive deoxygenation, thione-to-nitrile exchange, and nitrile hydrolysis—builds the unique pyrrolidine ring of proline. Multiple upstream routes to HCN (atmospheric photochemistry vs. formamide thermal decomposition), to formaldehyde (hydrothermal CO₂ reduction), and to NH₃ (hydrothermal outgassing vs. recycling from cyanosulfidic steps) create network redundancy and environmental interconnection. H₂S and NH₃ are catalytically recycled within the cyanosulfidic cycle, creating autocatalytic feedback loops.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H₂-rich serpentinization fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed on the greigite (Fe₃S₄) surface, which is structurally analogous to biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters. H₂ dissociates on the iron-sulfur surface, and hydride transfer to the adsorbed CO₂ reduces it to formate (HCOO⁻). The alkaline vent conditions favor formate stability.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate has been demonstrated as a key step in prebiotic carbon fixation, analogous to the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway in modern biology. This is well established in the iron-sulfur world hypothesis (Wächtershäuser, 1988; Roldan et al., 2015). Formate is the primary C1 product of hydrothermal CO₂ reduction and serves as an entry point for further organic synthesis.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: Formate adsorbed on the magnetite surface undergoes further reduction by H₂. The Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ redox couple in magnetite facilitates electron transfer, reducing the carboxylate to an aldehyde. This produces formaldehyde (HCHO), a critical C1 building block.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite surfaces produces a range of C1-C3 oxygenated products including formaldehyde from formate reduction (McCollom & Seewald, 2007). Formaldehyde is the essential feedstock for downstream aldol chemistry leading to acrolein and ultimately the C3 backbone of 3-aminopropanal.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formamide synthesis from CO₂, NH₃, and H₂

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, NH₃ from vent fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is first reduced to formate on the FeS surface. The formate then undergoes condensation with ammonia (NH₃) under hydrothermal conditions, forming formamide (HCONH₂) with loss of water. The FeS mineral surface promotes both the reduction and amidation steps.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide has been proposed as a key prebiotic intermediate that can be produced under hydrothermal conditions (Saladino et al., 2012). FeS/mackinawite is an effective catalyst for CO₂ reduction in alkaline hydrothermal systems (Roldan et al., 2015). Formamide serves as an alternative precursor to HCN via thermal decomposition.

reactions.name: Surface condensation of formate and ammonia to formamide

conditions.other: Dry heating phase of wet-dry cycle, evaporitic pool margin

reactions.mechanism: During the drying phase of evaporitic pools, formate and ammonia are concentrated on phosphate mineral surfaces. Thermal condensation drives amide bond formation between formate and ammonia, yielding formamide with loss of water. Phosphate minerals catalyze the dehydration condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Condensation of formate and ammonia to formamide is thermodynamically favorable under dehydrating conditions (Saladino et al., 2012). Phosphate minerals are known to promote condensation reactions in evaporitic settings. This provides an alternative surface route to formamide from hydrothermal-derived formate, creating a convergence point in the network.

reactions.name: Thermal decomposition of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: Subaerial geothermal field, mineral surface catalysis

reactions.mechanism: Formamide adsorbed on montmorillonite clay surfaces undergoes thermal dehydration to produce HCN and water. The clay mineral lowers the activation barrier for the elimination reaction by stabilizing the transition state through surface interactions.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decomposition of formamide on mineral surfaces (including clays and metal oxides) is a well-documented route to HCN and other prebiotic molecules including nucleobases (Saladino et al., 2012; Barks et al., 2010). This provides an alternative HCN source to atmospheric photochemistry, connecting hydrothermal formamide production to the surface cyanosulfidic pathway.

reactions.name: UV photochemical synthesis of HCN from N₂ and CH₄

conditions.other: Strong UV irradiation (no ozone layer), atmospheric/surface interface, anoxic atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: UV photons dissociate N₂ and activate CH₄ at the atmosphere-mineral interface. On TiO₂ photocatalyst surfaces, UV generates electron-hole pairs that drive N₂ fixation. Reactive nitrogen intermediates (N atoms, NH radicals) combine with methyl radicals from CH₄ photolysis to form HCN. Molecular hydrogen (H₂) is released as a byproduct.
reactions.reasoning: Atmospheric photochemistry of N₂/CH₄ mixtures producing HCN is a major source of prebiotically available cyanide, supported by both experimental and computational studies (Tian et al., 2011; Rimmer & Rugheimer, 2019). TiO₂ (anatase/rutile) is a well-known UV photocatalyst present on early Earth's surface. This route provides the primary atmospheric source of HCN that feeds into the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic chemistry.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde to acrolein

conditions.other: Evaporitic pool, base-catalyzed aldol chemistry, formose-related network

reactions.mechanism: Three equivalents of formaldehyde undergo sequential aldol condensation and dehydration on the montmorillonite surface. First, two formaldehyde molecules condense to glycolaldehyde; then a third formaldehyde adds via aldol reaction to give a C3 intermediate (glyceraldehyde or dihydroxyacetone), which dehydrates to yield acrolein (prop-2-enal). The clay surface concentrates reactants and catalyzes the aldol–dehydration sequence.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction network, which produces C2–C6 sugars and aldehydes from formaldehyde, is a well-established prebiotic pathway (Breslow, 1959; Ricardo et al., 2004). Acrolein is a known product of formaldehyde condensation under basic conditions. Montmorillonite clay catalyzes aldol condensations and concentrates organics in evaporitic settings. Patel et al. (2015) note that 3-aminopropanal can derive from acrolein + NH₃, placing this reaction upstream of their cyanosulfidic pathway.

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to acrolein forming 3-aminopropanal

conditions.other: Evaporitic pool, aqueous solution, ambient conditions

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia acts as a nucleophile in a 1,4-conjugate (Michael) addition to acrolein. The nitrogen lone pair attacks the β-carbon of the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde, yielding the 1,4-adduct 3-aminopropanal. The aldehyde functionality is preserved for subsequent Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation. Montmorillonite surface may pre-organize reactants and stabilize the product.
reactions.reasoning: Michael addition of ammonia to acrolein producing 3-aminopropanal is a well-known reaction in synthetic chemistry and is prebiotically plausible. Patel et al. (2015) identify 3-aminopropanal as the starting C3 amino aldehyde for their cyanosulfidic proline synthesis. The literature notes that 3-aminopropanal is itself a product of cyanosulfidic/surface chemistry from simple C1–C3 feedstocks.

reactions.name: Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation (3-aminopropanal + HCN)

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic evaporitic pool, ambient temperature and pressure

reactions.mechanism: Hydrogen cyanide (HCN) undergoes nucleophilic addition to the aldehyde carbonyl of 3-aminopropanal. The cyanide ion attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming a new C–C bond and yielding the α-hydroxynitrile (cyanohydrin) 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile. The free amine group on the 3-aminopropanal is preserved for subsequent cyclization. This extends the C3 aldehyde to the C4 aminonitrile chain length needed for pyrrolidine ring formation.
reactions.reasoning: Step 1 of the Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015) cyanosulfidic proline synthesis pathway. Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation is a classic prebiotic carbon–carbon bond forming reaction. The stibnite surface is consistent with the cyanosulfidic reaction environment where sulfide minerals are present.

reactions.name: H₂S-mediated thiolation and intramolecular cyclization

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions, H₂S atmosphere, ambient temperature

reactions.mechanism: Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) attacks the nitrile carbon of 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile, converting the C≡N group to a thioamide (C=S). The primary amine at the opposite end of the chain then undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic attack on the thioamide carbon, closing the five-membered pyrrolidine ring. This produces 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione with release of ammonia (NH₃). The released NH₃ is recycled back into upstream reactions (rxn_003, rxn_004, rxn_008).
reactions.reasoning: Step 2 of the Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015) pathway. This one-pot thiolation-cyclization is characteristic of cyanosulfidic chemistry, where H₂S activates nitriles toward cyclization. The simultaneous ring closure and thione formation is the key step that constructs the pyrrolidine ring unique to proline among proteinogenic amino acids.

reactions.name: Reductive deoxygenation of 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione (Cu/H₂S)

conditions.catalyst: elemental copper (Cu⁰) with H₂S as co-reductant
conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions, Cu⁰ deposits formed by Cu²⁺ reduction by H₂S in surface settings

reactions.mechanism: Reductive removal of the C3 hydroxyl group from 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione. The Cu/H₂S reducing system donates electrons to cleave the C–OH bond, producing the fully reduced pyrrolidine-2-thione with loss of water. Elemental copper acts as the primary reductant, with H₂S serving as a co-reductant and sulfur shuttle. Native copper is prebiotically plausible in surface settings where dissolved Cu²⁺ ions are reduced by H₂S.
reactions.reasoning: Step 3 of the Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015) pathway. The Cu/H₂S reducing system is central to the cyanosulfidic framework. Elemental copper is a plausible prebiotic reductant — copper deposits form when dissolved Cu²⁺ ions from weathered copper minerals are reduced by H₂S in geochemical settings. H₂S consumed here is resupplied by hydrothermal sources and regenerated in rxn_012.

reactions.name: Thione-to-nitrile exchange (pyrrolidine-2-thione + HCN)
reactions.mechanism: Cyanide displaces the thione sulfur at C2 of the pyrrolidine ring. HCN acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic thioamide carbon. The C=S bond is broken with release of H₂S, and a new C–CN bond is formed, yielding pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile. This reaction converts a sulfur-containing functional group to a nitrogen-containing one — a key interconversion in cyanosulfidic chemistry. The released H₂S is recycled for use in rxn_010 (thiolation-cyclization) and rxn_011 (reductive deoxygenation), creating a catalytic sulfur cycle.
reactions.reasoning: Step 4 of the Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015) pathway. The thione-to-nitrile exchange exemplifies the elegant interplay between sulfur and nitrogen functional groups in cyanosulfidic chemistry. H₂S recycling (consumed in Step 2, regenerated here in Step 4) is a hallmark of the Sutherland cyanosulfidic framework.

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis of pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile to L-Proline

conditions.catalyst: phosphate minerals (may catalyze hydrolysis via metal ion coordination)
conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic pool, ambient temperature, two equivalents of water consumed, pH-dependent kinetics

reactions.mechanism: Sequential hydrolysis of the nitrile group (C≡N) of pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile. Water attacks the nitrile carbon nucleophilically, forming a primary amide intermediate (pyrrolidine-2-carboxamide), which undergoes a second hydrolysis to the carboxylic acid with release of ammonia. Two equivalents of water are consumed. The product is L-Proline (racemic proline produced; L-form designated as the biological enantiomer). The released NH₃ is recycled into upstream reactions.
reactions.reasoning: Step 5 of the Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015) pathway. Nitrile hydrolysis at ambient temperature and neutral pH can be slow (days to weeks); the presence of metal ions from dissolved minerals may catalyze the hydrolysis. This completes the conversion of the nitrile to the carboxylate, yielding proline. As noted in the literature review, the chirality question remains open — the pathway produces racemic proline.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal carbon fixation to cyanosulfidic proline synthesis
pathways.description: Complete pathway from CO₂ through hydrothermal formate and formaldehyde production, surface aldol condensation to acrolein, Michael addition with NH₃ to 3-aminopropanal, then through all 5 steps of the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic route to L-Proline.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN photochemistry feeding cyanohydrin formation
pathways.description: HCN produced by UV photochemistry of N₂/CH₄ at the atmosphere-surface interface enters the cyanosulfidic pool and drives the Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation step, initiating ring construction toward L-Proline.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formamide to HCN to cyanosulfidic proline
pathways.description: Formamide synthesized from CO₂, NH₃, and H₂ on mackinawite in hydrothermal vents is transported to surface environments where thermal decomposition on montmorillonite produces HCN, which then feeds the full cyanosulfidic pathway.

pathways.name: Surface formate–ammonia condensation to formamide to HCN route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate is transported to surface evaporitic pools where it condenses with ammonia on phosphate minerals to form formamide. Formamide then thermally decomposes on clay to HCN, entering the cyanosulfidic proline pathway.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN thione-to-nitrile exchange branch
pathways.description: HCN from atmospheric photochemistry enters the cyanosulfidic pathway at the thione-to-nitrile exchange step (rxn_012), converting pyrrolidine-2-thione to pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile and completing proline synthesis. This highlights HCN's dual role in the network.

pathways.name: Catalytic H₂S sulfur cycle pathway
pathways.description: H₂S from hydrothermal vents is consumed in the thiolation-cyclization (rxn_010) and reductive deoxygenation (rxn_011) steps, then regenerated in the thione-to-nitrile exchange (rxn_012). This catalytic sulfur cycle drives continuous proline production from a limited H₂S supply.

pathways.name: Ammonia nitrogen recycling feedback loop
pathways.description: NH₃ released as a byproduct in thiolation-cyclization (rxn_010) and nitrile hydrolysis (rxn_013) is recycled back into the Michael addition of ammonia to acrolein (rxn_008), regenerating 3-aminopropanal. This nitrogen recycling loop makes the proline pathway partially autocatalytic in NH₃.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formamide to HCN for thione-to-nitrile exchange
pathways.description: Formamide produced hydrothermally (rxn_003) is decomposed on surface clay (rxn_005) to generate HCN that specifically feeds the thione-to-nitrile exchange step (rxn_012). This pathway highlights the hydrothermal contribution to the late-stage cyanosulfidic transformation.

pathways.name: Dual HCN source convergence pathway
pathways.description: HCN from both atmospheric photochemistry (rxn_006) and formamide thermal decomposition (rxn_005, from either hydrothermal rxn_003 or surface rxn_004 formamide sources) converges at the same cyanosulfidic pool, feeding both the cyanohydrin formation (rxn_009) and thione-to-nitrile exchange (rxn_012) steps simultaneously.

pathways.name: Integrated multi-environment proline synthesis
pathways.description: The complete integrated pathway combining all three environments: hydrothermal CO₂ fixation provides formate and formaldehyde (carbon backbone) plus H₂S (sulfur source) and NH₃ (nitrogen source); surface UV photochemistry provides HCN; surface cyanosulfidic chemistry on sulfide mineral surfaces constructs the pyrrolidine ring through the full Patel et al. (2015) 5-step sequence. All feedstock streams converge at the surface evaporitic pool where proline is assembled.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This full-length pathway faithfully implements the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic core (rxn_009–rxn_013) with hydrothermal upstream carbon fixation. The main weakness is rxn_007 (formaldehyde → acrolein), which compresses a multi-step aldol/dehydration sequence into a single step — acrolein (C3H4O) from formaldehyde (CH2O) requires at least aldol condensation to glycolaldehyde, further condensation, and dehydration. Additionally, rxn_011 lacks explicit Cu⁰ in the molecule node list despite being described as Cu/H₂S-mediated. Stoichiometry of rxn_013 should consume 2 H₂O.
suggestions: Decompose rxn_007 into at least two steps: (1) formose-type aldol condensation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde, and (2) aldol condensation + dehydration to acrolein
Add elemental copper (Cu⁰) as an explicit molecule/agent node for rxn_011
Correct rxn_013 stoichiometry to consume 2 H₂O

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses atmospheric UV photochemistry of N₂/CH₄ (well-established Titan-like chemistry) to supply HCN for the cyanohydrin step. Still depends on the problematic rxn_007 for acrolein/3-aminopropanal. This pathway only has 6 steps described but needs formaldehyde from somewhere — missing the explicit upstream source for 3-aminopropanal in this pathway description.
suggestions: Clarify the formaldehyde source within this pathway
Consider linking to hydrothermal formaldehyde production

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal formamide → thermal HCN → cyanosulfidic proline is a chemically cleaner upstream route than the formaldehyde/acrolein path. Formamide thermal decomposition to HCN is well-established. The hydrothermal formamide synthesis (rxn_003) is plausible but the one-step CO₂+NH₃+H₂ → formamide is simplified. Still needs 3-aminopropanal from another branch.
suggestions: Break rxn_003 into formate formation followed by amide bond formation
Explicitly state how 3-aminopropanal is obtained in this pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Routes through surface formate–ammonia condensation. rxn_004 should specify a mineral catalyst for the surface condensation. The formate + NH₃ → formamide step is thermodynamically unfavorable without activation energy input or catalysis. Still contains the problematic rxn_007.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalyst for rxn_004 (e.g., TiO₂ or montmorillonite)
Add thermal cycling or UV activation to drive amide bond formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a partial pathway (3 steps) providing atmospheric HCN for the thione-to-nitrile exchange. It does not independently produce proline — it is a branch that feeds into the main pathway. As a standalone pathway it is incomplete.
suggestions: This should be described as a branch/sub-pathway rather than a full pathway to target

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Correctly identifies the H₂S catalytic sulfur cycle (consumed in rxn_010, regenerated in rxn_012) as described in Patel et al. 2015. This is an important feature of the cyanosulfidic framework. However, as a 'pathway' it is really a sub-cycle within the main pathway, not a route to the target.
suggestions: Better framed as a catalytic cycle annotation rather than an independent pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Correctly identifies ammonia recycling: NH₃ is consumed in rxn_008 (Michael addition) and regenerated in rxn_010 (cyclization) and rxn_013 (hydrolysis). This autocatalytic feedback is a genuine feature of the cyanosulfidic network. The pathway as described shows the nitrogen economy well.
suggestions: Could be strengthened by quantifying NH₃ stoichiometry across the cycle

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A branch pathway supplying HCN from hydrothermal formamide for the thione-to-nitrile exchange step. Chemically sound but incomplete as a route to proline — only covers HCN supply and the last two transformations.
suggestions: Frame as a branch sub-pathway rather than an independent route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The dual HCN source convergence nicely demonstrates network redundancy: formamide thermal decomposition (rxn_005) and atmospheric photochemistry (rxn_006) both feed HCN into the cyanosulfidic steps. This creates robustness against failure of either HCN source. Still depends on 3-aminopropanal from the acrolein branch.
suggestions: Explicitly include the 3-aminopropanal supply branch in the pathway description

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The comprehensive integrated pathway that traverses all three environments (hydrothermal → surface → biochemical product). This is the most complete pathway and best represents the network's design intent. All 13 reactions contribute to an interconnected synthesis. The core cyanosulfidic steps (rxn_009–rxn_013) are excellent and directly supported by Patel et al. 2015. Upstream hydrothermal carbon fixation (rxn_001, rxn_002) and atmospheric HCN synthesis (rxn_006) provide realistic feedstock generation. Main weaknesses: rxn_007 (formaldehyde to acrolein oversimplified), rxn_011 missing explicit Cu⁰ agent, rxn_013 H₂O stoichiometry. Despite these, the overall pathway is scientifically sound and well-aligned with literature.
suggestions: Decompose rxn_007 into formose-type aldol steps (CH2O → glycolaldehyde → acrolein)
Add Cu⁰ as an explicit agent/molecule node for rxn_011
Fix stoichiometry of rxn_013 (2 H₂O consumed)
Address chirality — the network produces racemic proline, not specifically L-Proline

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed synthesis network that faithfully implements the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic proline synthesis as its core (rxn_009–rxn_013), and extends it with plausible upstream feedstock generation in hydrothermal (CO₂ fixation to formate/formaldehyde/formamide) and surface (atmospheric HCN photochemistry) environments. The network topology is strong: 18 molecules and 13 reactions with 5 well-connected hub molecules (HCN degree 4, NH₃ degree 5, H₂S degree 3, formate degree 3, formamide degree 3). Convergent pathways to HCN from two independent sources (atmospheric photochemistry and formamide decomposition) provide network robustness. The H₂S and NH₃ recycling loops correctly capture the autocatalytic features of cyanosulfidic chemistry. Literature alignment is excellent — all 5 core cyanosulfidic steps match Patel et al. 2015 precisely, with correct intermediates (4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile, 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione, pyrrolidine-2-thione, pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile). Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_007 (formaldehyde → acrolein) is oversimplified and chemically questionable as a single step — this is the weakest link in the network; (2) elemental copper for rxn_011 is not represented as a molecule node; (3) rxn_013 stoichiometry should consume 2 H₂O; (4) the network claims to produce L-Proline but the cyanosulfidic pathway produces a racemic mixture — chirality is not addressed; (5) rxn_004 lacks mineral specification for a surface reaction. Despite these issues, the network demonstrates strong chemical reasoning, excellent literature alignment, and meaningful environmental interconnection.
